author: @kongo289
text: What Happens In The Enclave, Doesn’t Happen Anywhere Else. A Country With Two Celebrations That Are Based On Ethic And Background Line. The Celebrations. 1) 1st July, this is the actual Independence Day of Rwanda, but the independence celebration day was scrapped by Criminal Paul Kagame. Currently, it is forbidden and a crime to celebrate anything on this day. However, Rwandans in Diaspora who lived in Rwanda pre-1994 still celebrate this day. Within Rwanda’s dirty and backward politics it’s called Hutu day, because it’s mostly Hutus who feel attached to this day. 2) 4th July, criminal Paul Kagame created another day of celebration, 4th July, known as Liberation day, (referred in Rwanda as Kwibohora day). This is an official public holiday, and celebrations are encouraged within the Country. Within Rwanda’s dirty and backward politics it’s called Tutsi day, because it’s mostly Tutsis who feel attached to this day and most especially those Rwandans who returned back to their country in post 1994. Ladies and gentlemen, this is the Rwanda under Criminal Paul Kagame, a Rwanda that the brutal ruler has failed to unite under a particular purpose and goal, a Rwanda that ethnic underline are merely underplayed rather than being reconciled and solved.

author: @denisepaul263
text: Physically speaking, all countries are beautiful, with mountains, lakes, rivers, forests etc. But are they still beautiful when plantations are plundered, reared animals are stolen, houses of innocent people are set on fire, students are abducted by criminal gangs, political leaders are incompetent and corrupt, houses not getting water and electricity? I am black myself and I consider that it would be a very big mistake if all the flaws of a country were ignored. It is only when our mistakes are brought forward that there is amelioration. According to my observation, we africans keep blaming others, especially west, for our plight. There is a very long time ago since all african nations have got their independence, have they made good use of it? Apartheid has been abolished in Zimbabwe and South Africa, how is the situation there? Time has come to stop blaming others and try to address our own issues. There must be an outcry when something is wrong without us Killing each other and applaud any positive action. Nevertheless I say Hats off to Rwanda who has shown that despite years of war and suffering, they have been able to stabilise their country and make it a better place, it is an inspiration for other african countries.

author: @shadowpawn
text: Kigali was an amazing experience only time I went. From most modern airport to cleanest city I've been to in my life. Umuganda is a well-known practice that is conducted every last Saturday of the month, and takes root from the Rwandan culture of seeking solutions to our problems, and cooperation- It was the first step to not only a clean city, but also a clean country, clean households and an emphasis on personal hygiene for every citizen. Since 2009, Rwandan people in neighborhoods or in their respective homes come together to work on a specific project; it can be cleaning a public garden, helping someone fix their home (especially during rainy season where houses may get damaged) to planting trees. . Kigali Genocide Memorial Centre still is the most pain in a space I've experienced.

author: @michaelseibold9977
text: We just got back from two weeks in Rwanda and what a country! We absolutely loved it! From the chaos of Kigali streets with the motorbikes and the moving Genocide Memorial to the stunning national parks - Nyungwe, Volcanoes and Akagera - as well as Lake Kivu, every sight and sound is amazing. And the people are so kind and loving. Sitting only a meter away from a momma gorilla and her babies and having a Silverback brush by me was one of the great experiences of my life. And all of the animals in the Akagera savannah! Elephant, zebra, hippo, lion, antelope, baboon, the thousands of birds and so many more. Rwanda is a place you must visit!

author: @PinnacleJourneys-c1h
text: Rwanda is a country of absolutely captivating and lush natural magnificence — earning its name as the Land of a Thousand Hills with breathtaking justification. Rolling green hills blanketed in tea plantations, dense mist-covered forests, shimmering lakes, and dramatic volcanic peaks create a landscape of such extraordinary beauty that every single scene feels like a painting. From the ancient and mystical Volcanoes National Park, home to the magnificent and endangered mountain gorillas whose presence fills every visitor with a sense of profound privilege and wonder, to the vast and tranquil waters of Lake Kivu — one of Africa's Great Lakes — reflecting the surrounding hills in a mirror of pure serenity, to the lush and biodiverse Nyungwe Forest, one of the oldest rainforests in Africa, alive with chimpanzees, colobus monkeys, and over 300 species of birds — every single frame of your video was a breathtaking testament to Rwanda's natural glory. You captured the soul and heartbeat of this incredible land with such artistry and passion!

Its president is known to have studied the works of Chairman Mao and used some of those ideas to guide Rwanda’s transformation. As a Chinese person, I have never read Chairman Mao’s books myself, and I did not think his ideas had such strong practical influence abroad. Seeing Rwanda’s development has made me realize that I may have underestimated the impact of his thinking.

A few days ago, a western diplomat in Uganda commented that the country's roads, full of potholes, provide a unique and enjoyable experience. And I'm not making this up, it even trended on Twitter. How sad is that!

author: @dannytesfay649
text: Discover the allure of Rwanda - a second home that beckons with its pristine landscapes, unwavering safety, and vibrant greenery. Amidst it all, you'll find affordability seamlessly intertwined with a warmth that resonates in the kindness of its people. Picture a country where cleanliness meets serenity, and safety is not just a promise but a way of life. Rwanda stands as a haven of tranquility, inviting you to immerse yourself in its rich tapestry. What sets Rwanda apart is not just the lush greenery or the affordable prices but the genuine beauty that radiates from its people. Their kindness transcends borders, creating an atmosphere that makes you feel not just welcome but truly at home. Come, visit Rwanda, and embark on an unparalleled experience. From the breathtaking landscapes to the smiling faces, every moment promises to be a memory etched in your heart. Rwanda awaits, assuring you a stay like no other. Rwanda💕

author: @lydian5762
text: Steven, when he said 10 000$ he is actually close to the truth and that is for a Rwandese in Diaspora going there for at least a month. It includes high end restaurants, /car rentals ( that can run you easily 100$/day) alternatively you may use the uber( via VW) and a ride from kibagabaga to downtown is already about 10$ one way so you can see how transportation can be really expensive. I do not take bikes or moto bikes but this alone would have probably saved me 1000$ while there. Kigali is really expensive because of all the expats and tourism they receive, outside Kigali it is a whole different vibe and way cheaper and almost like a different country all together.

text: Kigali really stands out for how calm, clean, and organized it feels, especially for solo travelers. It’s refreshing to see an African city that prioritizes safety, walkability, and quality of life. This kind of honest perspective is so important for people considering travel or relocation.

author: @nicole_mylene
text: So many activities to do in Rwanda😭😭 so sad you didn't get to experience them; ziplining in Nyungwe (which is the longest zip line in East Africa), canopy walk in Nyungwe as well, the akagera national park to see the big 5 and so many other animals, visit the kings palace and see the royal cows, there are so many lakes to visit with great food and so many activities like kayaking. Try the street food in nyamirambo car free zone. There are so many other malls as well with arcades and many more fun activities. You could go to fazenda for horse riding, shooting traditional Rwandan arrows. So many restaurants to explore, most brunches also offered traditional Rwandan food😭😭. There are so many volcanoes and mountains to hike with great views. So many things to do in Rwanda. Whenever you come back to Rwanda try these. I'm sure there's so many more that I didn't even get to mention. Welcome again to Rwanda!

author: @XY-rh3if
text: NO! There's not much to do in Rwanda. I'm a foreigner here myself. However, there're many beautiful clean cities and villages to visit, and the amazing landscape all across Rwanda makes it very intresting. In addition, the woman are uniquely beautiful but watch out for those woman who just want to dine out at your expense and move on. After all, you wouldn't sign any agreement in advance to do anything beyond dinning them out at your expense anyway. The best thing about Rwanda for me is that Rwandans are the most well behaved people that I've ever come across, and I've lived in 4 other countries including visited 6 others. Normally, I fall in love with a new country but this is the first time that I fell in love with the people primarily, then the country secondarily.

author: @iguillo
text: I've been watching you all's channel for a few months now, and am so glad you visited Rwanda! One of my best friends lives there and she invited me to visit three years ago. I'd never been to the continent and so Rwanda became the first African country I've ever visited. What an experience it was! Like you all mentioned, Kigali is one of the CLEANEST cities I've ever been to, in such a beautiful country. I've been twice in the last three years and plan to go back soon. It's too bad you all didn't get a chance to experience the rest of the country like Akagera National Park, Volcanos National Park, or Lake Kivu. But, hopefully it inspires you to return. By the way, I've been to Heaven Restaurant / Hotel as well and found it just as awesome as you all did. I've been more than once! LOL!😃😃

text: We are Rwandans. We are the last warning of how deeply a country can be destroyed — but also the strongest example of how quickly a nation can rise again. From pain, we chose unity.
